authoritative_sources.title: HIPAA Eligibility Transaction System (HETS) 270/271
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public payer or provider operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS operates Medicare eligibility transaction infrastructure. HETS directly supports eligibility evidence, 270/271 terminology, and the distinction between active coverage evidence and full service readiness.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: eligibility verification and coverage evidence capture

authoritative_sources.title: CMS Interoperability and Prior Authorization Final Rule (CMS-0057-F)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS-0057-F is federal public policy for prior authorization APIs, status transparency, and denial-reason communication by impacted payers. It grounds authorization status workflows without authorizing this lane to approve or interpret payer decisions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: authorization status handling and payer dependency escalation
used_for: conflicting public baseline and tenant policy scenario

authoritative_sources.title: Managed Care Appeals and Grievances: Appeals Overview
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public payer or provider operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS managed care appeals and grievances guidance is authoritative for Medicare health plan complaint, organization determination, and appeal pathways. It supports escalation boundaries when guest-service complaints become formal payer or appeal work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: complaint, grievance, organization determination, reconsideration, and appeal owner routing

authoritative_sources.title: Provider requirements and resources under the No Surprises Act
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS provider guidance for the No Surprises Act governs transparency, good faith estimate, patient-provider dispute, and billing-protection context relevant to guest-service billing and service recovery communications.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: billing-sensitive guest communication and patient-provider dispute routing

authoritative_sources.title: HIPAA Minimum Necessary Standard
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, Office for Civil Rights
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR is the HIPAA privacy regulator. Minimum-necessary guidance directly constrains PHI collection, internal routing, stakeholder summaries, and escalation packets in service-resolution work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: privacy-gated intake, evidence packaging, and stakeholder communication

authoritative_sources.title: Uses and Disclosures for Treatment, Payment, and Health Care Operations
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, Office for Civil Rights
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR guidance explains permitted use and disclosure of PHI for treatment, payment, and health care operations, including health-plan customer service, while requiring role-based access and other limits.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: patient/member communication, proxy/requester handling, and operations-service routing

authoritative_sources.title: CAQH CORE Operating Rules
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CAQH CORE is designated by HHS as the national operating rule authoring entity for HIPAA-covered administrative transactions. Its rules ground eligibility, claim status, payment/remittance, prior authorization, referrals, attachments, acknowledgements, and error handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: eligibility, claim status, remittance, prior authorization, referral, attachment, and acknowledgement workflow

authoritative_sources.title: CAHPS Health Plan Survey
authoritative_sources.publisher: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AHRQ CAHPS Health Plan Survey is a national standard for measuring consumer experience with health plans, including getting needed care, getting care quickly, and health plan customer service, making it authoritative for patient/member service terminology and failure modes.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: patient/member service experience, complaint classification, and repeat-failure escalation framing

authoritative_sources.title: National Performance Goal #1: Right Patient, Right Care
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Joint Commission is a recognized healthcare accrediting body. Its right-patient/right-care focus supports identity and service-matching escalation when guest-service work could route or communicate about the wrong patient.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: patient identity, arrival readiness, handoff, and patient-safety escalation

workflow_questions.question: What prerequisites or authorizations gate the workflow?
workflow_questions.answer: Minimum prerequisites include patient/member identity, requester authority, consent or proxy status, approved communication channel, service date, facility, service setting, payer and plan, eligibility status, benefits context, referral and authorization requirements, encounter or discharge context, documentation dependencies, travel/accommodation owner, service recovery authority, and tenant source hierarchy.

workflow_questions.question: Which records or systems establish the patient, member, or claim truth set?
workflow_questions.answer: Truth-set systems include EHR or EMR, master patient index, registration and scheduling systems, practice management, payer portals, real-time eligibility systems, referral and authorization systems, utilization review, case management, CRM, contact-center, patient/member access systems, privacy/consent systems, revenue-cycle systems, claim/remittance/denial/appeal systems, and tenant medical-travel support tools. Tenant source hierarchy decides conflicts.

workflow_questions.question: Where does the lane stop and licensed clinical, privacy, or payer-interpretation authority begin?
workflow_questions.answer: The lane stops before diagnosis, treatment planning, clinical triage, discharge instruction changes, care-plan changes, medical necessity, utilization review judgment, authorization approval, payer policy interpretation, appeal strategy, coding assignment, claim correction, privacy-release approval, legal interpretation, financial approval, and safety-critical clearance.

workflow_stages.name: Service intake and boundary classification
workflow_stages.description: Normalize guest, patient, member, family, visitor, medical-traveler, or care-team request intent; verify requester authority, identity, channel, service date, facility, payer, plan, and requested action before treating the request as actionable.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Prerequisite and system-of-record retrieval
workflow_stages.description: Retrieve eligibility, benefits, referral, authorization, encounter, discharge, care-transition, charge, claim, denial, appeal, CRM, complaint, PHI, consent, and medical-travel records from authoritative systems.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Fulfillment or troubleshooting disposition
workflow_stages.description: Classify whether the request can be resolved, is blocked on prerequisites, needs clarification, or must be handed off; document policy, payer, PHI, clinical, safety, financial, travel, or service recovery blockers.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance

workflow_stages.name: Stakeholder communication and evidence capture
workflow_stages.description: Prepare guest-facing or stakeholder-safe communication summaries with minimum-necessary PHI handling, evidence traceability, unresolved items, caveats, and next-owner status.
source_classes: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Policy, authority, complaint, and safety escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate policy exceptions, repeat failures, formal grievances, billing disputes, payer interpretation, clinical questions, PHI uncertainty, patient-safety signals, identity mismatch, and service recovery requests outside delegated authority.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

artifacts: Patient/member identifiers, MRN, member ID, requester identity, relationship, proxy or consent records, communication preferences, and privacy restrictions
Eligibility and benefits records, 270/271 responses, payer/plan identifiers, referral records, prior authorization status, authorization numbers, payer portal status, and attachment acknowledgements
Encounter documentation, orders, clinical-administrative notes, discharge summaries, care-transition records, case-management notes, and utilization review records
Appointment, arrival, transportation, lodging, accommodation, visitor support, medical-travel, and concierge handoff records
Charge capture, coding-status flags, claim records, remittance records, denial reasons, appeal documentation, and revenue-cycle routing notes
CRM cases, contact-center transcripts, service tickets, complaint logs, prior service recovery, guest profile notes, approved message templates, and stakeholder communication summaries
Resolution notes, request-resolution summaries, exception communication escalation notes, handoff summaries, low-confidence notes, and audit records
systems_of_record: EHR or EMR and associated clinical documentation systems
Master patient index, registration, scheduling, and patient access systems
Practice management, revenue cycle, claim, remittance, denial, and appeal systems
Real-time eligibility, benefits verification, payer portal, referral, authorization, and utilization review systems
Case management, care transition, discharge planning, CRM, contact-center, complaint, and patient/member communication systems
Consent, proxy, privacy restriction, role-based access, audit, and identity verification systems
Medical travel, accommodation, transportation, visitor support, service recovery, and hospitality guest profile systems where tenant-authorized

allowed: Classify requests as in scope, out of scope, blocked, informational, exception-driven, complaint-driven, or owner-transfer sensitive.
Request minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, and source context needed to continue safely.
Summarize documented eligibility, referral, authorization, encounter, discharge, travel, claim, or complaint status without interpreting clinical or payer meaning.
Apply documented service or policy rules when records and delegated authority support the resolution.
Prepare resolution notes, customer communication summaries, exception escalation notes, and handoff packages with evidence references.
escalate: Required patient/member, payer, plan, facility, service date, referral, authorization, encounter, discharge, claim, privacy, or tenant policy facts are missing or conflict.

The request involves PHI disclosure uncertainty, proxy or consent ambiguity, identity mismatch, patient-safety risk, clinical judgment, medical necessity, payer policy interpretation, authorization approval, coding, claim correction, denial appeal, refund, waiver, compensation, or legal authority.
A formal complaint, repeat service failure, accessibility issue, transportation/lodging safety issue, or service recovery request exceeds documented authority.
Public baseline guidance and tenant policy, payer contract, facility rule, supplier policy, or jurisdictional requirement materially conflict.
refuse: Disclose PHI when requester authority, approved channel, consent, proxy status, role-based access, or minimum-necessary purpose is unresolved.
Diagnose, triage, change discharge instructions, recommend treatment, approve medical necessity, or imply licensed clinical judgment.

Approve or deny authorization, interpret payer policy as binding, override payer prerequisites, assign codes, correct claims, draft appeals, approve refunds, or grant compensation outside delegated authority.
Bypass identity, eligibility, referral, authorization, documentation, privacy, patient-safety, service recovery, or financial approval controls to improve guest satisfaction.

domain_failure_modes: the lane treats active eligibility as service clearance even though referral or authorization dependencies remain missing
PHI is disclosed to a family member, visitor, supplier, travel partner, or employer without verified requester authority and minimum-necessary controls
a guest-service script promises diagnosis, treatment, medical necessity, discharge instruction changes, or clinical safety clearance
a payer denial or authorization status is reinterpreted as a guest-service resolution instead of being routed to utilization management, payer operations, or appeals ownership
a billing, coding, claim, remittance, denial, or appeal request is handled as a hospitality service recovery issue instead of revenue-cycle or coding work
a repeat interpreter, wheelchair escort, transportation, or lodging failure is closed with a generic apology instead of complaint escalation and evidence capture
identity or MRN mismatch is bypassed to keep a medical traveler's arrival schedule moving
tenant medical-travel policies are applied without payer, facility, service-date, consent, and source-of-truth retrieval
the overlay collapses into generic hotel concierge support and omits healthcare-specific eligibility, authorization, PHI, clinical, payer, and documentation boundaries

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Missing authorization, referral, or eligibility dependency
PHI or privacy-sensitive request that requires controlled handling
Boundary case that would require licensed clinical or payer-interpretation escalation
Customer or stakeholder requests a policy exception
Service request lacks the detail needed for safe resolution
Communication becomes a handoff to another lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Hospitality-and-travel label conflicts with the queue's resolved healthcare-and-care-delivery profile.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack treats hospitality-and-travel as guest-facing service vocabulary and medical-travel/concierge operating context, while the healthcare profile controls records, PHI, payer, clinical, and safety boundaries.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific lodging, transportation, refund, service recovery, interpreter, visitor, and medical-travel program rules vary by facility, payer product, jurisdiction, and contract.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Runtime must retrieve tenant service model, facility policy, payer product, delegated authority thresholds, supplier contracts, and jurisdiction before applying property- or travel-specific outcomes. The lane escalates rather than guessing.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: State privacy, consent, minor/proxy rules, charity-care policies, and payer appeal rights may be stricter than the public federal baseline.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The package leaves no blocking ambiguity because it requires retrieval and controlled escalation whenever jurisdiction or tenant policy changes the answer.

notes: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated specification-based package.
Research output constrains scenarios to prerequisite handling, PHI control, clinical and payer boundary handling, policy exception escalation, handoff packaging, and tenant adaptation.
